one_line_takeaway: FATH flips prompt-injection defense from 'ignore injected instructions' to 'authenticate the legitimate answer with per-query HMAC hash tags,' reaching 0% attack success on GPT-3.5 (and residual single-digit-to-30% success on Llama3 under adaptive attack) at a modest clean-utility cost.
key_finding: On OpenPromptInjection+ (Table 2), FATH achieves the lowest ASR of all methods across every attack and injection task on both models. On GPT-3.5 it drives ASR to 0.00 for all five Threat-Model-1 attacks AND for the Adaptive Attack across URL/QA/CLF. On Llama3 it reaches near-0% under Threat Model 1 (e.g., 0.00 URL/QA under Context Ignoring) but the adaptive attack is only reduced, not eliminated (0.26/0.34/0.31 URL/QA/CLF). On InjecAgent direct-harm (Table 3, 510 examples) FATH gives 0.00 ASR under both Combined and Adaptive attacks vs 99.3 (Llama3) / 1.00 (GPT-3.5) with no defense. Against the gradient-based optimization attack on Llama3, FATH holds 0% ASR vs 70% with no defense. Ablation (Table 4) shows both components matter, with the Security Policy being the more critical: removing it raises adaptive-attack ASR by >30% (to 0.34/0.38/0.56), while removing tags gives 0.06/0.07/0.18.
core_contribution: FATH (Formatting AuThentication with Hash-based tags) is a test-time defense against indirect prompt injection in LLM-integrated applications. Its novelty is an inversion of the standard defense paradigm: instead of prompting the LLM to IGNORE instructions found in external text (which attackers defeat via contradictory 'ignore previous instructions' injections), FATH lets the LLM answer ALL received instructions but forces it to label each response as authorized or unauthorized inside secret, per-query HMAC hash-based tags; a rule-based parser then returns only the response wrapped in the authorized-response tag (TAG4). Because the authorization tags are freshly HMAC-generated per query and never exposed to the attacker, the attacker cannot forge the tag needed to have its output pass verification. The paper also contributes OpenPromptInjection+, an extended benchmark adding general Stanford-Alpaca instructions and three injection-task categories (URL, QA, CLF) on top of the original OpenPromptInjection.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection: the attacker can only manipulate the EXTERNAL text/tool-output that the application feeds to the LLM; it cannot see or alter the user instruction, the formatting templates, or (for the black-box settings) the model parameters/logits. Attack goal is to make the LLM follow the injected instruction instead of the user's. The paper defines three escalating settings: (1) Threat Model 1 - attacker unaware of the defense, using five off-the-shelf attacks (Naive, Escape Characters, Context Ignoring, Fake Completion, Combined); (2) Threat Model 2 - adaptive attacker who knows all defense details (e.g., can inject fake </data> tags to spoof boundaries) but still cannot predict the per-query secret hash tags; (3) an optimization-based worst case where the attacker additionally has white-box access to model parameters and input prompts and uses gradient-based prompt injection (Liu et al. 2024), still restricted to editing only the external text.
limitations: Author-stated: (1) substantial manual effort to hand-craft defense prompts per application, evidenced by the very different templates needed for OpenPromptInjection+ vs InjecAgent; (2) reliance on strong instruction-following ability - effectiveness would drop on weaker models such as Alpaca; (3) benchmarks lack real tool execution, so external text is fed in to SIMULATE tool outputs. Reviewer-observed from the paper's own tables: robustness is uneven across models - on Llama3 the adaptive attack still succeeds ~26-34% of the time, so the headline '0% ASR' claim holds mainly for GPT-3.5; and FATH consistently lowers the clean Judge Score (Llama3 6.73 vs 8.31 no-defense; GPT-3.5 6.91 vs 7.94), a utility cost the authors attribute to filtering out reasoning content.

datasets_used: OpenPromptInjection+ (this paper's extension of OpenPromptInjection; 100 Stanford-Alpaca examples per injection task for each of URL, QA, CLF)
Stanford Alpaca (source of general user instructions + external text, using 'instruction' and 'input' fields)
QA injection questions from Zverev et al. 2024
5 of 7 OpenPromptInjection classification tasks (sentiment, spam, hate, duplicate-sentence, NLI)
InjecAgent (Zhan et al. 2024) direct-harm subset, 510 examples
quantitative_results: OpenPromptInjection+ GPT-3.5: FATH ASR = 0.00 across all five Threat-Model-1 attacks (URL/QA/CLF) and 0.00/0.00/0.00 under Adaptive Attack
OpenPromptInjection+ Llama3: FATH Adaptive-Attack ASR = 0.26/0.34/0.31 (URL/QA/CLF); Context-Ignoring ASR 0.00/0.00/0.06
InjecAgent (510 direct-harm examples): FATH = 0.00 ASR under Combined and Adaptive attacks; No-Defense = 99.3 (Llama3) and 1.00 (GPT-3.5)
Optimization-based (gradient) attack on Llama3: FATH 0% ASR vs 70% ASR with no defense (using Expectation-Over-Time over 5 tag sets)
Clean Judge Score: FATH 6.73 (Llama3) / 6.91 (GPT-3.5) vs No-Defense 8.31 / 7.94
Ablation on GPT-3.5 Adaptive Attack (URL/QA/CLF): FATH 0.00/0.00/0.00; w/o Security Policy 0.34/0.38/0.56; w/o Authentication Tags 0.06/0.07/0.18; No Defense 0.60/0.98/0.72
